revision.comment: 島根県の式内社に大麻山神社を追加。
revision.text:
{{神社| 名称=大麻山神社| 画像={{画像募集中|cat=浜田市}}| 所在地=島根県浜田市三隅町室谷1097| 祭神=天日鷲命| 社格=式内社（小）・郷社}}
'''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町の神社。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[郷社]]。
[[神宮寺]]として尊勝寺があり、西の[[高野山]]と呼ばれていたが、[[天保]]七年（[[1836年|1836]]）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（[[1872年|1872]]）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。
== 祭神 == 祭神は[[天日鷲命]]・[[猿田彦命]]・大麻彦命（[[フトダマ|太玉命]]）。[[蔵王権現]]・[[熊野権現]]・[[伊豆山権現|走湯権現]]・[[山王権現]]・[[白山権現]]を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。
== 歴史 == 大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。[[仁和]]四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。[[寛平]]元年（[[889年|889]]）[[阿波国]]（[[徳島県]]）[[板野郡]]大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、[[宇多天皇]]の勅許により社殿を建立した。
== 伝説 == 阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて[[忌部氏|忌部族]]が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の[[高山 (萩市)|高山]]まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>
『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。
== 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『式内社調査報告 第二十一巻 山陰道４』皇学館大学出版部、1983 * 谷川健一編『日本の神々―神社と聖地 第七巻 山陰』白水社、 1985年 * 寺井実郎編『三隅地方の神話と伝説』三隅時報社、1960 * 酒井董美、萩坂昇『出雲・石見の伝説 日本の伝説48』角川書店、1980 * 島根県史編纂掛編『島根縣史 第２巻（国造政治時代）』名著出版、1972 * 三隅町誌編さん委員会『三隅町誌』1971
== 補注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == [[島根県の観光地]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.genbu.net/data/iwami/ooasa_title.htm 玄松子の記憶]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おおあさやま}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:浜田市の建築物]] [[Category:浜田市の歴史]] [[Category:石見国の式内社]] [[Category:島根県の旧郷社]]

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

revision.comment: 3 revisions imported
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=大麻山神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=浜田市}} |所在地=[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町室谷1097 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神=天日鷲命 |社格=[[式内社]]（小）・旧[[郷社]] |地図= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=大麻山神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町の神社。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[郷社]]。
[[神宮寺]]として尊勝寺があり、西の[[高野山]]と呼ばれていたが、[[天保]]七年（[[1836年|1836]]）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（[[1872年|1872]]）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。
== 祭神 == 祭神は[[天日鷲命]]・[[猿田彦命]]・大麻彦命（[[フトダマ|太玉命]]）。[[蔵王権現]]・[[熊野権現]]・[[伊豆山権現|走湯権現]]・[[山王権現]]・[[白山権現]]を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。
== 歴史 == 大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。[[仁和]]四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。[[寛平]]元年（[[889年|889]]）[[阿波国]]（[[徳島県]]）[[板野郡]]大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、[[宇多天皇]]の勅許により社殿を建立した。
== 伝説 == 阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて[[忌部氏|忌部族]]が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の[[高山 (萩市)|高山]]まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>
『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。
== 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『式内社調査報告 第二十一巻 山陰道４』皇学館大学出版部、1983 * 谷川健一編『日本の神々―神社と聖地 第七巻 山陰』白水社、 1985年 * 寺井実郎編『三隅地方の神話と伝説』三隅時報社、1960 * 酒井董美、萩坂昇『出雲・石見の伝説 日本の伝説48』角川書店、1980 * 島根県史編纂掛編『島根縣史 第２巻（国造政治時代）』名著出版、1972 * 三隅町誌編さん委員会『三隅町誌』1971
== 補注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == [[島根県の観光地]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.genbu.net/data/iwami/ooasa_title.htm 玄松子の記憶]

{{神社 |名称=大麻山神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=浜田市}} |所在地=[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町室谷1097 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神=天日鷲命 |社格=[[式内社]]（小）・旧[[郷社]] |地図= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=大麻山神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町の神社。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[郷社]]。
[[神宮寺]]として尊勝寺があり、西の[[高野山]]と呼ばれていたが、[[天保]]七年（[[1836年|1836]]）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（[[1872年|1872]]）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。
== 祭神 == 祭神は[[天日鷲命]]・[[猿田彦命]]・大麻彦命（[[フトダマ|太玉命]]）。[[蔵王権現]]・[[熊野権現]]・[[伊豆山権現|走湯権現]]・[[山王権現]]・[[白山権現]]を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。
== 歴史 == 大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。[[仁和]]四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。[[寛平]]元年（[[889年|889]]）[[阿波国]]（[[徳島県]]）[[板野郡]]大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、[[宇多天皇]]の勅許により社殿を建立した。
== 伝説 == 阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて[[忌部氏|忌部族]]が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の[[高山 (萩市)|高山]]まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>
『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。
== 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『式内社調査報告 第二十一巻 山陰道４』皇学館大学出版部、1983 * 谷川健一編『日本の神々―神社と聖地 第七巻 山陰』白水社、 1985年 * 寺井実郎編『三隅地方の神話と伝説』三隅時報社、1960 * 酒井董美、萩坂昇『出雲・石見の伝説 日本の伝説48』角川書店、1980 * 島根県史編纂掛編『島根縣史 第２巻（国造政治時代）』名著出版、1972 * 三隅町誌編さん委員会『三隅町誌』1971
== 補注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

== Japanese Wikipedia content == {{神社 |名称=大麻山神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=浜田市}} |所在地=[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町室谷1097 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神=天日鷲命 |社格=[[式内社]]（小）・旧[[郷社]] |地図= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=大麻山神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町の神社。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[郷社]]。
[[神宮寺]]として尊勝寺があり、西の[[高野山]]と呼ばれていたが、[[天保]]七年（[[1836年|1836]]）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（[[1872年|1872]]）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。
== 祭神 == 祭神は[[天日鷲命]]・[[猿田彦命]]・大麻彦命（[[フトダマ|太玉命]]）。[[蔵王権現]]・[[熊野権現]]・[[伊豆山権現|走湯権現]]・[[山王権現]]・[[白山権現]]を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。
== 歴史 == 大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。[[仁和]]四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。[[寛平]]元年（[[889年|889]]）[[阿波国]]（[[徳島県]]）[[板野郡]]大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、[[宇多天皇]]の勅許により社殿を建立した。
== 伝説 == 阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて[[忌部氏|忌部族]]が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の[[高山 (萩市)|高山]]まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>
『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。
== 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『式内社調査報告 第二十一巻 山陰道４』皇学館大学出版部、1983 * 谷川健一編『日本の神々―神社と聖地 第七巻 山陰』白水社、 1985年 * 寺井実郎編『三隅地方の神話と伝説』三隅時報社、1960 * 酒井董美、萩坂昇『出雲・石見の伝説 日本の伝説48』角川書店、1980 * 島根県史編纂掛編『島根縣史 第２巻（国造政治時代）』名著出版、1972 * 三隅町誌編さん委員会『三隅町誌』1971

== Japanese Wikipedia content == {{神社 |名称=大麻山神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=浜田市}} |所在地=[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町室谷1097 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神=天日鷲命 |社格=[[式内社]]（小）・旧[[郷社]] |地図= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=大麻山神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は[[島根県]][[浜田市]]三隅町の神社。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[郷社]]。
[[神宮寺]]として尊勝寺があり、西の[[高野山]]と呼ばれていたが、[[天保]]七年（[[1836年|1836]]）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（[[1872年|1872]]）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。
== 祭神 == 祭神は[[天日鷲命]]・[[猿田彦命]]・大麻彦命（[[フトダマ|太玉命]]）。[[蔵王権現]]・[[熊野権現]]・[[伊豆山権現|走湯権現]]・[[山王権現]]・[[白山権現]]を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。
== 歴史 == 大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。[[仁和]]四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。[[寛平]]元年（[[889年|889]]）[[阿波国]]（[[徳島県]]）[[板野郡]]大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、[[宇多天皇]]の勅許により社殿を建立した。
== 伝説 == 阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて[[忌部氏|忌部族]]が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の[[高山 (萩市)|高山]]まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>
『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。
== 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『式内社調査報告 第二十一巻 山陰道４』皇学館大学出版部、1983 * 谷川健一編『日本の神々―神社と聖地 第七巻 山陰』白水社、 1985年 * 寺井実郎編『三隅地方の神話と伝説』三隅時報社、1960 * 酒井董美、萩坂昇『出雲・石見の伝説 日本の伝説48』角川書店、1980 * 島根県史編纂掛編『島根縣史 第２巻（国造政治時代）』名著出版、1972 * 三隅町誌編さん委員会『三隅町誌』1971
== 補注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == [[島根県の観光地]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.genbu.net/data/iwami/ooasa_title.htm 玄松子の記憶]

== 伝説 == 阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて{{jalink|忌部氏|Q8189229|忌部族}}が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の{{jalink|高山 (萩市)|Q11669293|高山}}まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>
『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

[[JA:大麻山神社]]

{{wikidata link|Q30925653}}

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称=大麻山神社
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=浜田市}}
|所在地={{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|浜田市|Q432055}}三隅町室谷1097
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神=天日鷲命
|社格={{jalink|式内社}}（小）・旧{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}
|地図= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=大麻山神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|浜田市|Q432055}}三隅町の神社。{{jalink|式内社}}で旧社格は{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}。

{{jalink|神宮寺|Q1074971}}として尊勝寺があり、西の{{jalink|高野山|Q535065}}と呼ばれていたが、{{jalink|天保|Q1369045}}七年（{{jalink|1836年|Q7601|1836}}）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（{{jalink|1872年|Q7753|1872}}）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。

=== 祭神 ===
祭神は{{jalink|天日鷲命}}・{{jalink|猿田彦命}}・大麻彦命（{{jalink|フトダマ|Q11059539|太玉命}}）。{{jalink|蔵王権現|Q10514059}}・{{jalink|熊野権現|Q11568925}}・{{jalink|伊豆山権現|Q11380566|走湯権現}}・{{jalink|山王権現|Q11470054}}・{{jalink|白山権現|Q11579578}}を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。

=== 歴史 ===
大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。{{jalink|寛平|Q1346292}}元年（{{jalink|889年|Q23833|889}}）{{jalink|阿波国|Q907457}}（{{jalink|徳島県|Q160734}}）{{jalink|板野郡|Q1156025}}大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、{{jalink|宇多天皇|Q349267}}の勅許により社殿を建立した。

=== 伝説 ===
阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて{{jalink|忌部氏|Q8189229|忌部族}}が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の{{jalink|高山 (萩市)|Q11669293|高山}}まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>

『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称=大麻山神社

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|浜田市|Q432055}}三隅町の神社。{{jalink|式内社}}で旧社格は{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}。

{{jalink|神宮寺|Q1074971}}として尊勝寺があり、西の{{jalink|高野山|Q535065}}と呼ばれていたが、{{jalink|天保|Q1369045}}七年（{{jalink|1836年|Q7601|1836}}）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（{{jalink|1872年|Q7753|1872}}）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。

=== 祭神 ===
祭神は{{jalink|天日鷲命}}・{{jalink|猿田彦命}}・大麻彦命（{{jalink|フトダマ|Q11059539|太玉命}}）。{{jalink|蔵王権現|Q10514059}}・{{jalink|熊野権現|Q11568925}}・{{jalink|伊豆山権現|Q11380566|走湯権現}}・{{jalink|山王権現|Q11470054}}・{{jalink|白山権現|Q11579578}}を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。

=== 歴史 ===
大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。{{jalink|寛平|Q1346292}}元年（{{jalink|889年|Q23833|889}}）{{jalink|阿波国|Q907457}}（{{jalink|徳島県|Q160734}}）{{jalink|板野郡|Q1156025}}大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、{{jalink|宇多天皇|Q349267}}の勅許により社殿を建立した。

=== 伝説 ===
阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて{{jalink|忌部氏|Q8189229|忌部族}}が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の{{jalink|高山 (萩市)|Q11669293|高山}}まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>

『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称=大麻山神社

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''大麻山神社'''（おおあさやまじんじゃ、たいまさんじんじゃ）は{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|浜田市|Q432055}}三隅町の神社。{{jalink|式内社}}で旧社格は{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}。

{{jalink|神宮寺|Q1074971}}として尊勝寺があり、西の{{jalink|高野山|Q535065}}と呼ばれていたが、{{jalink|天保|Q1369045}}七年（{{jalink|1836年|Q7601|1836}}）、長雨による地すべりのため、尊勝寺、大麻山神社ともに倒壊した。さらに、明治五年（{{jalink|1872年|Q7753|1872}}）の浜田地震で被災し、廃仏毀釈の時代であったことも影響して尊勝寺は再建されることなく廃寺となった。

=== 祭神 ===
祭神は{{jalink|天日鷲命}}・{{jalink|猿田彦命}}・大麻彦命（{{jalink|フトダマ|Q11059539|太玉命}}）。{{jalink|蔵王権現|Q10514059}}・{{jalink|熊野権現|Q11568925}}・{{jalink|伊豆山権現|Q11380566|走湯権現}}・{{jalink|山王権現|Q11470054}}・{{jalink|白山権現|Q11579578}}を配祀し五社権現と呼ばれるようになった。

=== 歴史 ===
大麻山は双子山と呼んでいた。{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}四年（888）御神託により大麻山と改め朝廷に奏聞した。{{jalink|寛平|Q1346292}}元年（{{jalink|889年|Q23833|889}}）{{jalink|阿波国|Q907457}}（{{jalink|徳島県|Q160734}}）{{jalink|板野郡|Q1156025}}大麻山鎮座の大神を勧請し、{{jalink|宇多天皇|Q349267}}の勅許により社殿を建立した。

=== 伝説 ===
阿波国から大麻比古命に率いられて{{jalink|忌部氏|Q8189229|忌部族}}が移住してきた。大麻比古命は海を馬で渡り、山を舟で行った。大麻山に入った大麻比古命は周辺の小野の原に住んでいた小野族めがけ岩を投げつけて追い払った。須佐の{{jalink|高山 (萩市)|Q11669293|高山}}まで逃げた小野族は反撃して岩を投げたが大麻山の麓までしか届かなかった。やがて仲裁に立つ者が現れて小野族は小野に戻り、忌部族は大麻山を領分とすることで話が決着した。<ref>「次の『三隅地方の神話と伝説』（三隅時報社／編）に掲げられている『大麻山と高山の合戦』という一文は著者の故寺井実郎氏が史実を系統づけ乍ら実地踏査をして書いたものであって、神話をうまく歴史に織りこんで書かれているので、故人の冥福を祈りつつここに掲載することにする」と三隅町誌にある。</ref>

『島根縣史』では「太古井野村と大麻村とに戰ひが始まつて天馬が馳せ廻つた其の跡が印しつけられた石といふのが大麻山の中腹に今でも残って居る」とある。

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) · Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} ({{jalink|1836|Q7601|1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753|1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{jalink|Zaō Gongen|Q10514059}}, {{jalink|Kumano Gongen|Q11568925}}, {{jalink|Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{jalink|Sannō Gongen|Q11470054}}, and {{jalink|Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{jalink|Kanpyō|Q1346292}} ({{jalink|889|Q23833|889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{jalink|Awa Province|Q907457}} ({{jalink|Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734}}), {{jalink|Itano District|Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{jalink|Emperor Uda|Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229|Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{jalink|Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) · Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} ({{jalink|1836|Q7601|1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753|1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{jalink|Zaō Gongen|Q10514059}}, {{jalink|Kumano Gongen|Q11568925}}, {{jalink|Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{jalink|Sannō Gongen|Q11470054}}, and {{jalink|Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{jalink|Kanpyō|Q1346292}} ({{jalink|889|Q23833|889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{jalink|Awa Province|Q907457}} ({{jalink|Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734}}), {{jalink|Itano District|Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{jalink|Emperor Uda|Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229|Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{jalink|Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō

|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) · Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} ({{jalink|1836|Q7601|1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753|1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{jalink|Zaō Gongen|Q10514059}}, {{jalink|Kumano Gongen|Q11568925}}, {{jalink|Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{jalink|Sannō Gongen|Q11470054}}, and {{jalink|Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{jalink|Kanpyō|Q1346292}} ({{jalink|889|Q23833|889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{jalink|Awa Province|Q907457}} ({{jalink|Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734}}), {{jalink|Itano District|Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{jalink|Emperor Uda|Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229|Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{jalink|Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985

* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) · Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} ({{jalink|1836|Q7601|1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753|1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{jalink|Zaō Gongen|Q10514059}}, {{jalink|Kumano Gongen|Q11568925}}, {{jalink|Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{jalink|Sannō Gongen|Q11470054}}, and {{jalink|Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{jalink|Kanpyō|Q1346292}} ({{jalink|889|Q23833|889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{jalink|Awa Province|Q907457}} ({{jalink|Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734}}), {{jalink|Itano District|Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{jalink|Emperor Uda|Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229|Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{jalink|Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō

|Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) · Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{jalink|Hamada City|Q432055}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} ({{jalink|1836|Q7601|1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753|1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{jalink|Zaō Gongen|Q10514059}}, {{jalink|Kumano Gongen|Q11568925}}, {{jalink|Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{jalink|Sannō Gongen|Q11470054}}, and {{jalink|Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{jalink|Kanpyō|Q1346292}} ({{jalink|889|Q23833|889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{jalink|Awa Province|Q907457}} ({{jalink|Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734}}), {{jalink|Itano District|Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{jalink|Emperor Uda|Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229|Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{jalink|Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill||lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===
* Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 21, San’indō 4*, Kōgakkan University Press, 1983
* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].
