== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō

|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
|Name=Ōasayama Shrine
|Image={{Image requested|cat=Hamada City}}
|Location={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} 1097 Murodani, Misumi-chō
|Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|Enshrined deity=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto
|Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) · Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}
|Map= {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Ōasayama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”

=== References ===

* Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *The Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 7: San’in*, Hakusuisha, 1985
* Jitsurō Terai (ed.), *Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region*, Misumi Jihōsha, 1960
* Masumi Sakai, Noboru Hagisaka, *Legends of Izumo and Iwami: Japanese Legends 48*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1980
* Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office (ed.), *History of Shimane Prefecture, Volume 2 (Era of Provincial Governance)*, Meicho Publishing, 1972
* Misumi Town History Compilation Committee, *History of Misumi Town*, 1971

{{nihongo|'''Taimasan Shrine'''|大麻山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

'''Ōasayama Shrine''' (おおあさやまじんじゃ, also read たいまさんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Misumi-chō, {{ill|lt=Hamada City|Q432055|WD=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}.

There was a Buddhist temple, Sonshō-ji, serving as the shrine-temple ({{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}), and it was called the “Kōyasan of the West.” However, in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1836|Q7601|WD=Q7601|lt=1836|1=1836}}), due to landslides caused by prolonged rainfall, both Sonshō-ji and Ōasayama Shrine collapsed. Furthermore, they were damaged in the Hamada earthquake of the fifth year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}). Influenced by the era of anti-Buddhist policies (haibutsu kishaku), Sonshō-ji was never rebuilt and became a ruined temple.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hiwashi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|1=Futodama}}). In addition, {{ill|lt=Zaō Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Izusan Gongen|Q11380566|WD=Q11380566|lt=Hashiyu Gongen|1=Izusan Gongen}}, {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}} are jointly enshrined, and the shrine came to be called the Five-Gongen Shrine.

=== History ===
Ōasayama was originally called Twin Mountains. In the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} (888), following a divine oracle, it was renamed Ōasayama, and this was reported to the imperial court. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|lt=889|1=889}}), the great deity enshrined at Mount Ōasa in {{ill|lt=Awa Province|Q907457|WD=Q907457|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}} ({{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}), {{ill|lt=Itano District|Q1156025|WD=Q1156025|1=Itano District, Tokushima}}, was invited, and with the imperial permission of {{ill|lt=Emperor Uda|Q349267|WD=Q349267|1=Emperor Uda}}, a shrine building was constructed.

=== Legend ===
Led by Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto, the {{ill|lt=Imbe clan|Q8189229|WD=Q8189229|lt=Imbe tribe|1=Inbe clan}} migrated from Awa Province. Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto crossed the sea on horseback and traveled through the mountains by boat. Upon entering Ōasayama, Ōasa-hiko-no-Mikoto drove away the Ono clan, who lived on the nearby Ono Plain, by throwing rocks at them. The Ono clan fled as far as {{ill|lt=Takayama (Hagi City)|Q11669293|WD=Q11669293|lt=Takayama|1=Mount Kōyama}} in Susa and retaliated by throwing rocks, but they only reached the foothills of Ōasayama. Eventually, a mediator appeared, the Ono clan returned to Ono, and the dispute was settled with the Imbe clan taking Ōasayama as their territory.<ref>“The passage titled ‘The Battle of Ōasayama and Takayama,’ included in the following ‘Myths and Legends of the Misumi Region’ (edited by Misumi Jihōsha), was written by the late Terai Jitsurō, who conducted on-site investigations while systematizing historical facts. As it skillfully weaves myth into history, it is reprinted here while praying for the repose of the deceased,” as stated in the Misumi Town History.</ref>

In the “History of Shimane Prefecture,” it is written: “In ancient times, a battle began between Ino Village and Ōasa Village, and stones bearing the marks of where heavenly horses galloped about still remain today on the mountainside of Ōasayama.”
